title: The cross-border effects of bank capital regulation
abstract: We study the international coordination of bank capital requirements under a host-country rule: the requirement depends on where the borrower, not the bank, is located. In such a regime, countries compete for scarce bank equity capital. Raising a country’s requirement may generate bank capital outflows as well as inflows. We pin down the condition for the sign of the capital flow and the associated externality, and highlight the policy implications. Absent collaboration, overshooting is likely: individual countries have an incentive to increase Basel III’s Counter-Cyclical Capital Buffer too much in good times and cut it too much in bad times.

author.Saleem Bahaj: University College London, London, United Kingdom|||Bank of England, London, United Kingdom|||CEPR, London, United Kingdom
author.Frederic Malherbe*: University College London, London, United Kingdom|||CEPR, London, United Kingdom

article.1. Introduction: International standards for bank capital regulation have evolved over the past four decades from a simple, common 8% minimum capital requirement to a broad, complex toolkit. Macroprudential considerations have been a driving force behind this evolution. Prudential risks and macroeconomic cycles differ across countries, which suggests the need for heterogeneous and time-varying capital requirements. Under the current regime (Basel III) the stance of macroprudential regulation is effectively set at the discretion of national regulators via so-called countercyclical buffers (CCyBs). These buffers apply to all bank loans made in the regulator’s jurisdiction, irrespective of which jurisdiction the bank belongs to. Accordingly, we study a model in which capital requirements follow a host-country rule: they are set by the country in which lending takes place.The existing literature has studied home-country rules under which the requirement is set by a bank’s home regulator regardless of where lending takes place. In these circumstances, international competition for credit market share within a country is a key driver of strategic incentives: Regulators can give an advantage to banks in their jurisdiction by cutting capital requirements as this allows them to operate at a cheaper cost than banks from other jurisdictions. So, to the extent that regulators care about the profits of the banks they regulate, they have an incentive to undercut one another in order for their banks to steal market share (Dell’Ariccia and Marquez, 2006). Common minima eliminate this market-share externality. But, as we have outlined, national regulators have discretion over the stance of macroprudential requirements, and regulators and policymakers regularly express concerns about the international spillovers of capital requirements in the current regime. Yet there is little agreement on what the relevant externalities are and a formal framework for assessing them is needed.1We find that, under a host-country rule, what matters is not competition for market share but competition for bank capital. Key is how much equity capital is allocated to lending in different jurisdictions. Changes in capital requirements alter this allocation and effectively generate bank equity capital flows. We propose a model to study such capital flows and their implications for strategic interactions between regulators. The model has two dates and two countries (Home and Foreign), in which banks finance loans with a mix of insured deposits and equity capital. Risk-shifting incentives make banks endogenously specialise in lending in either country (so, we refer to them as Home or Foreign banks). These banks face capital requirements set by the regulator of the country in which the lending takes place. Bank equity capital is mobile and there is global competition for it.Our contribution is threefold: First, we show that, perhaps against conventional wisdom, an increase in the capital requirement in a country does not necessarily generate outflows of bank equity capital – inflows are possible too. We pin down the conditions under which either case occurs. Second, as bank equity capital is scarce, changes to capital requirements in a country impose, through capital flows, an externality onto the other country. We show that this capital flow externality is central to the incentive for national regulators to deviate from a collaborative optimum. If higher requirements generate positive externality, there is an incentive to deviate downwards, i.e., to undercut the other country (and vice versa if the externality is negative). Third, we point out the implications for the coordination of macroprudential capital regulation. In particular, under a host-country rule and absent coordination, macroprudential capital requirements are likely to be raised too much in good times and cut too much in troubled times, when bank equity capital is particularly scarce.To understand these results, let us expose the main mechanism of the model, starting from the perspective of a single country, Home. There are diminishing returns to capital in the economy, which translates into a downward sloping demand for loans. Imagine Home bank equity capital is fixed. Then, an increase in capital requirement will contract lending and raise its marginal return. If initial quantities are relatively high, this fall in lending increases profitability. The intuition is the same as uncompetitive firms in a product market boosting their profits by reducing quantities. However, if initial quantities are relatively low a fall in lending reduces profits; in the same way that collusive firms would lose profit if they reduced quantities past the point a monopolist would choose. Due to this mechanism, the Home banks’ profits and their return on equity are hump-shaped in the level and lending, and hence, in the capital requirement. If the capital requirement is initially below the return maximising level, then an increase will raise equity returns (and, vice versa, it will lower returns if we start above the return maximising level).Now take a cross-border perspective. In our model, banks issue equity competitively to investors who supply it at a cost increasing in the total amount raised across countries. Hence, there is a global supply curve for equity. Investors face no constraints on where they invest and, in equilibrium, the return on bank equity is equal across countries (and to the cost of raising equity). Imagine a change in capital requirements at Home that increases the Home bank return on equity. Home banks respond by demanding more equity. This shifts the equilibrium along the global supply curve. Hence, Foreign banks face an inward shift in their residual supply curve. There is a higher equilibrium cost of equity, which results in a contraction in lending in Foreign.2 Such a spillover is at the heart of our analysis.We interpret this spillover as a capital flow. To understand why, consider an extreme case where there is a fixed global supply of capital. All capital flowing into Home must flow out from Foreign; we have a 100% spillover. Conversely, if the global supply is perfectly elastic, all capital flowing to Home will be newly raised capital and there is no spillover. For intermediate cases, part of the equity capital increase in Home can be seen as being siphoned off from Foreign.Intuitively, the direction of the equity capital flow following an increase in the Home capital requirement depends on whether the Home bank return on equity increases or decreases. If there is an increase, equity capital flows into Home, if there is a decrease, capital flows out. We can now link back to the single-country perspective described above: here too, whether or not the return on equity increases depends precisely on whether the initial capital requirement is above or below the return maximising level. If Home capital requirements are below the return maximising level this implies that the initial level of lending is relatively high. Then a tighter requirement restrains the quantity of lending at Home which raises profits and hence the Home bank return on equity. So capital flows out of Foreign banks. (Vice versa, if initial requirement is high, lending is low, tighter requirements lower Home returns and cause a capital flow to Foreign banks).Having characterised this mechanism for a given set of capital requirements, we then turn to a policy game in which we endogenise the requirements. Regulators seek to maximise net output subject to deadweight losses from financial instability. Bank equity capital is socially valuable as it alleviates such losses. But banks prefer to operate with maximum leverage so there is role for capital regulation. As we have seen, regulation has crossborder effects which generates strategic incentives. We compare the Nash equilibrium to collaboration. Nash regulators effectively compete for bank equity capital.When a tighter capital requirement attracts equity capital to Home, some of it is siphoned off from Foreign. There is a negative externality; part of the cost of tighter regulation is borne abroad. In contrast, if tighter requirements lead to an outflow from Home, Foreign benefits. Under Nash competition, regulators ignore these effects. As a result, whether they have an incentive to deviate upwards or downwards from the collaborative outcome depends on the sign of the capital flows the deviation would generate.Which case arises depends on parameter values. When equity capital is abundant, collaboration can achieve a high level of lending while preserving financial stability with relatively tight requirements. Given that the collaborative level of lending is high, we are in the region where marginally increasing the capital requirement raises profitability. Tighter regulation therefore generates inflows, the incentive to deviate is upward, and the Nash equilibrium corresponds to a race-to-the-top scenario. In contrast, when equity capital is relatively scarce, the level of lending is low. Looser requirements therefore generate inflows and the incentive is to undercut the other regulator. The Nash equilibrium of the policy game corresponds to a race-to-the-bottom scenario.Our analysis suggests that competing regulators will raise the CCyB too high in normal times (when bank capital is not too scarce).3 In this case, there are gains from coordinating on more modest raises. However, if bank capital is very scarce (think of bad or troubled times, for instance after a big negative shock to bank equity capital), then competing regulators will have an incentive to cut the CCyB by more than what collaboration requires.Ceteris paribus, a larger weight put on financial stability in the welfare function implies a lower collaborative level of lending and therefore a higher likelihood that it is in the region where the incentive to deviate is downward. Hence, the race-to-the-bottom scenario becomes more likely after a negative shock that would raise the potential cost of financial instability (for instance, a shock to fiscal capacity).The reference paper for a home-country rule is Dell’Ariccia and Marquez (2006). To illustrate how, in such a regime, regulators’ strategic interactions fundamentally differ from those in the one we study, it is best to highlight a key mechanism in their paper. They have a representative bank based in each of two countries, but both banks operate in both countries. Each bank has a fixed amount of equity capital and faces the capital requirement imposed by their country of origin. A key point is that a decrease in capital requirement by the Home regulator decreases the cost of capital for the Home-based bank, which gives it a competitive advantage in both markets and allows it to grab market share from its Foreign competitor. This is an externality that naturally gives incentives for countries to undercut one another. Adopting a reciprocity regime kills such a market-share externality.4In a similar international context, a series of papers study the interaction between capital regulation and other policy levers. Acharya (2003) looks at how discretion in resolution regimes can undermine the benefit of coordination in capital regulation. Morrison and White (2009) examine the link between banking regulation and supervisory quality. In their set up, capital requirements are a substitute to the regulator’s ability to distinguish sound banks from weak ones. Competition among regulators creates a selection effect: high quality banks prefer to be chartered by high ability regulators, which also set lower capital requirements than low ability regulators.5In addition, a sequence of papers have focused on international coordination of bank supervision, rather than capital requirements. Carletti et al. (2020) and Colliard both consider the role of central and local supervision when local supervisors have informational advantages but neglect cross-border externalities. Similarly, Calzolari and Loranth (2011) and Calzolari et al. (2018) consider how the presence of multinational banks alter supervisory incentives.At a more general level, Korinek (2016) identifies conditions under which there is scope for international policy collaboration. The relevant one in our paper is the violation of the Tinbergen Principle: Regulators have a single tool (the capital requirement) and face a policy trade off (e.g. between economic activity and financial stability). If regulators could, for instance, directly and costlessly subsidise the allocation of bank equity to domestic lending, then the trade off could disappear, and so could the gains from collaboration.In practice, fiscal policy is mostly separated from macro-prudential policy. How these policies should be coordinated within and between countries is a complex, multifaceted question. Government guarantees generate implicit subsidies that can distort bank behaviour and have direct fiscal implications that can spillover across borders when banks fail. Faia and Weder (2016), Bolton and Oehmke (2018) and Segura and Vicente (2019) study how the resolution of banks should be coordinated between countries. Moreover, monetary policy can directly affect bank profitability, which may also call for coordination across borders.6 Modelling all these different aspects of policy is beyond the scope of this paper, we focus on a prudential policymaker that only controls the capital requirement.Finally, even though our main focus is on strategic interactions between national regulators, other dimensions of our analysis directly relate to previous literature on capital requirements. Commonalities include moral hazard due to government guarantees (Kareken and Wallace, 1978), incentives for banks to specialise (e.g., Martinez-Miera and Suarez (2014), Harris et al. (2023), Bahaj and Malherbe (2020) and Malherbe and McMahon (2024)), and the study of optimal capital regulation in the face of policy trade offs (e.g., Begenau (2020), Malherbe (2020) and Elenev et al. (2021)).
article.2. The model: The model has two dates: 0 and 1. Decisions are made at date 0. At date 1, all stochastic variables are realised, and production and consumption take place.We consider two sovereign countries: Home and Foreign. There is a single, tradeable good which can be consumed or used as physical capital in production, in which case the goods depreciate fully. This good is also the numeraire.In each country, there is a representative firm, a representative household, a representative investor, and a regulator. There is also a mass of banks that can operate in both countries. We describe here the details of the environment in the Home country. The Foreign country has the same environment (although we do not necessarily impose symmetry in parameter values); foreign variables are marked with a .Agents and preferences.Private agents only value date-1 consumption, are risk neutral, and act competitively. We define and discuss regulator preferences starting in Section 4.Firms and technology.The representative firm operates a Cobb–Douglas production technology: , where  is physical capital,  is labour, , and  is a random variable that captures TFP. The firm invests in capital at date 0. At date 1,  realises and the firm hires labour and produces.We normalise  and assume that  is distributed over , with a corresponding density function , which is smooth, unless otherwise specified. Our analysis does not require to impose any specific structure on the dependence between  and  but we assume that the joint distribution has full support over .The firm starts penniless and borrows from banks to invest in physical capital. In equilibrium, it makes zero profits in all states. We therefore abstract from firm ownership.All agents have access to a riskless storage technology with a zero rate of return.Households.The representative household is endowed with one unit of labour, which it supplies inelastically (and, for simplicity, without disutility) at date 1. It also has an endowment of goods at date 0, which it initially allocates between insured bank deposits and the storage technology. We assume that this endowment is sufficiently large that the storage technology is always used in equilibrium. This pins down a households’ opportunity cost of funds of unity.Banks.Banks start penniless, issue equity (protected by limited liability), take deposits that are insured at no cost, and lend to firms subject to a capital requirement constraint that we define below. Banks can potentially lend in both countries. There is free entry, and equity and deposits can be raised globally (i.e. from investors and households in either country). Banks can freely choose their country of incorporation, which determines which taxpayer insures its deposits. Deposit insurance is funded by ex-post lump-sum taxes on households.Capital requirements: host country rule.Loans made in Home face a capital requirement  that is set by the Home regulator. Likewise, the Foreign regulator sets , which applies to loans made in Foreign. There is only one type of regulatory capital for banks: equity. To avoid confusion with physical capital, we henceforth refer to it as bank equity capital.Consider a bank  and denote  and  the quantity it lends in Home and Foreign, respectively, and denote its equity capital . This bank faces the following constraint, (1)In Section 3,  and  are treated as exogenous parameters, but from Section 4 they are the choice variables of a policy game.Banks could also invest in storage which would carry a zero capital requirement. However, raising deposits to invest them in storage would have no impact on the bank’s net cash flows, so we abstract from this in what follows.Investors and the supply of bank equity capital.That bank equity capital is scarce is a key ingredient of our analysis. For tractability reasons, many papers in the literature (e.g., Dell’Ariccia and Marquez (2006)) assume that the supply is fixed. However, that bank equity scarcity depends on the state of the economy seems the premise for the need of time varying capital requirements (Kashyap and Stein (2004), Kashyap et al. (2008), Malherbe (2020)). So, we adopt a more flexible approach and, following Hellmann et al. (2000), we assume a generally upward sloping supply curve. We formalise it as follows.Only the agents that we call investors can invest in bank equity. They are endowed with some initial wealth and can generate additional date-0 wealth, at a disutility cost. Specifically, to generate  units of wealth, an investor has to incur a disutility cost , with (2)and where  is a parameter and  is the global additional wealth generated by investors. Investors take the unit disutility cost as given. Generating additional wealth is costly and investors only potentially do so for the purpose of investing in bank equity, accordingly, we have: where  is the aggregate amount invested in bank equity globally, and  is the initial global endowment of investor wealth. Investors supply equity competitively and freely across borders. Therefore, function (2) constitutes the global bank equity inverse supply curve.The allocation of  between the representative investor in each country will not play a role in our analysis (and neither does the initial split of ). To rule out uninteresting cases where equity is fully abundant, we impose: .Market equilibrium.In an equilibrium: given capital requirements  and , banks and firms maximise expected pure profits (i.e., revenue minus the total costs, including capital); households and investors maximise utility (i.e. date-1 consumption less any disutility of wealth generation in date 0); and markets clear.Preliminaries.The problems of the firms, the households, and the investors are trivial. Price taking behaviour implies that, in equilibrium:•Deposits promise a zero net rate of return.•The wage is given by: , where  is aggregate capital. Given labour supply is normalised to 1, this corresponds to the aggregate wage bill.•The loan gross interest rate is , where  is aggregate lending.7•Investors break even in expectation. So, in equilibrium,  is such that the expected return on bank equity equals its marginal cost, i.e., the unit disutility of generating additional wealth .Market clearing requires:•.The banks’ problem is more involved and is the focus of the next section. However, parameter restrictions  ensure that banks will never be 100% safe. This allows us to focus attention on equilibria where capital requirements have interesting effects.
article.3. Positive analysis: equity capital flo: In this Section, we focus on the equilibrium behaviour of banks for a given pair of capital requirements. We treat  and  as parameters, and study how a small change in  affects the equilibrium allocation.3.1. The banks’ problem and the market equilibriumLemma 1(i) Banks default with strictly positive probability in equilibrium; (ii) capital requirements are binding; and (iii) each individual bank perfectly specialises as either a lender in Home or Foreign.ProofAll proofs are in Appendix C. □Bank shareholders break even in expectation. Depositors also break even, but when a bank defaults they do so at the taxpayer’s expense. Since deposits are in effect subsidised by the taxpayer, they are seen by the bank as cheaper than equity. This is why the capital requirement binds.8Government guarantees also induce banks, ceteris paribus, to minimise diversification. Such guarantees endow the bank with a valuable put option on their realised net worth (Merton, 1977). The value of this option is maximised when all of the bank’s loans generate negative cash flows (net of repayments to depositors) in the same state of the world (Harris et al., 2023). Hence, in equilibrium, it is optimal for banks to specialise in lending in just one country.9In practical terms, this means banks prefer to lend via separate balance sheets in each country (i.e. subsidiaries) rather than lend via branches (or engage in direct cross-border lending), which would diversify risk in a single balance sheet. Accordingly, one interpretation of our setup is that banks set up holding companies that operate across borders through separate subsidiaries in each country. Alternatively, we can think of individual banks as stand-alone specialised lenders in each country. The former is closer to how banks operate in reality. However, for ease of exposition, and to save on notation, we will present our analysis using the interpretation of banks as stand-alone specialised lenders in each country.Additionally, the market for bank equity capital is global, deposits promise a zero return in both countries, capital requirements follow a host-country rule, and there are no corporate taxes. Hence, banks are indifferent regarding their country of incorporation. This plays no role for their behaviour and so we only distinguish banks along the dimension of where they lend. We impose a tie-breaking rule that banks incorporate in, and issue deposits that are insured by, the country where they lend.The key object: Equity capital allocated to lending.The key object in our analysis will be , the aggregate quantity of bank equity capital allocated to banks specialising in lending at Home. A change in  causes a reallocation of equity capital between Home ( and Foreign (): effectively a bank equity capital flow. It turns out that the direction of this capital flow governs the strategic interactions between regulators. We will come on to this later. In this section we first look at the economic mechanisms that pin down the direction of the capital flow in the market equilibrium.Bank return on equity.We consider a representative bank specialising in lending to Home firms. We denote this bank’s equity . Since capital requirements bind, the bank’s lending is , and the proceeds from lending are given by  where the bank takes  as given. To lend an amount , the bank raises a total of  of deposits on which it pays zero interest. Aggregating across banks we have .Define  as the realisation of  such that the bank just has sufficient proceeds from its loans to make depositors whole. That is Given Lemma 1, the problem of the bank boils down to: (3)The second term reflects the cost of equity, where banks take  as given. Shareholders are protected by limited liability. So, the first term is their expected payout.Shareholders have collectively invested equity capital , hence, their expected return on equity is: (4)Market equilibrium.Let  denote an equilibrium level of Home capital.Proposition 1For all , there exists a unique pair  such that returns on equity in both countries are equal to the investors’ required return. This pair is implicitly defined by: (5)For intuition, note that, for a given , an increase in  implies more lending and therefore more aggregate physical capital. From diminishing returns, it directly follows that  is decreasing in  (and  in ). This leads to the result since  is weakly increasing, and strictly from .10In our interpretation that banks are stand-alone entities that specialise in lending in either country, international capital mobility means that they can raise equity capital from investors in either country. In the alternative bank holding company interpretation, one can think of the holding company raising equity (mainly or even exclusively) where it is incorporated, and then allocating it to its subsidiaries via its internal capital markets. Either way, Eq. (5) is the relevant indifference condition. That deposits are mobile internationally plays no role in our analysis. All results would, for instance, go through if deposits could only be raised in the country where a bank lends.3.2. International spilloversWe now turn to how capital requirements alter the allocation of bank equity capital to either Home or Foreign.Starting from equilibrium, we now consider the effect of marginal changes in the Home capital requirement . The no-arbitrage condition in Proposition 1 implicitly defines a function . The direction of international bank equity flowsLemma 2 and Proposition 2 show that the direction of equity capital flows hinges on the sign of . We now discuss the economics of this sign and establish that for any  there is a threshold value for  that pins down the direction of the capital flow.3.3.1. An increase in  can raise return on equity ()Given that  embeds a subsidy from a government guarantee, which is decreasing in the capital requirement, it is perhaps natural to assume that  is negative and higher capital requirements always generate capital outflows. However, there is another force via which increases in  can raise bank return on equity. Raising  decreases aggregate lending in Home, which can increase total revenues in the banking sector, much as if there had been a decrease in competition. Crucially, increased revenues translate to a higher return on equity. We first formalise this intuition with a simplified example and then come on to how things play out in our model.A simplified problem.Consider our environment, but without uncertainty (set ) and consider a fictitious monopolist bank, with predetermined capital , that maximises pure profits. The risk of bank failure implies that capital requirements are binding and banks specialise in equilibrium. From a normative perspective, it also motivates the use of capital requirements as policy tools. However, the introduction of uncertainty creates a technical challenge linked to the truncation in Eq. (10). To deal with this, we introduce the following condition.Condition 1 Regularity - Single CrossingFor all ,  when .The fundamental drivers of a hump-shaped return on equity in our simple example is that: (i) the production function is concave, which generates a downward sloping and convex loan demand curve; (ii) capital requirements bind. The regularity condition ensures  is a well-behaved hump shape in . This allows us to streamline the analysis by ignoring knife-edge cases where  could be locally positive.11Theorem 1Assume Regularity Condition 1 holds. Then, , there exists a unique  such that,  The threshold  can be interpreted as the capital requirement that maximises  given . When banks are risky, the implicit subsidy from deposit insurance modifies the shape of the supply of funds curve in Fig. 1, but its presence does not affect the fundamental logic: a tighter capital requirement still rotates the supply curve anticlockwise and  is still hump shaped in .In equilibrium banks cannot benefit from an increase in . is the value of the capital requirement that maximises the equilibrium return on equity. But, in any equilibrium, the return on equity is equated to the cost of raising equity (): if a tighter requirement raises returns it must also raise costs. Investors are always indifferent at the margin.Put differently,  does not maximise bank pure-profits , simply because the latter includes the cost of bank equity capital. In our model, perfect competition implies that banks’ pure profits are always zero in equilibrium, so a cost increase associated with a change in  just offsets the increase in revenue. Although an increase in  only makes the banks of our model weakly worse off, it would make them strictly worse off if they had market power (which is the case in reality). We show this in Appendix B.1.
article.4. The policy game: From now on, we treat  and  as the choice variables of national regulators who take as given that, in equilibrium: (i) capital requirements are binding; and (ii) market forces will equate bank return on equity across countries (per Proposition 1).4.1. Objective functions and solution conceptTo start, our baseline assumption is that the excess cost of equity () is private and is not internalised by regulators. Whether bank capital is socially costly is a subject of debate in the banking literature (Admati et al., 2013). In our context, assuming costs are private helps with tractability and allows us to isolate the key externality that we wish to focus upon. However, as we will discuss, the presence of this externality and our main results do not hinge on this assumption.We endow the Home regulator with the objective function (11) where  is a parameter.With the specification in Eq. (11), we consider a trade off between economic activity and financial stability. The first term, , captures the expected economic surplus generated by Home banks’ lending (recall ). To model the financial stability motive, we impose a deadweight cost of bank defaults in the second term that is proportional to the expected shortfall between bank liabilities and its assets in the event of default. In more compact notation, which will be useful to express partial derivatives, this shortfall reads: The mechanisms we study here do not hinge on the specific form of . As we shall see, its key features lie in the sign of its partial derivatives:  and , which we argue are quite natural.First, : at a given , an increase in  increases social costs of bank insolvency. This is natural for two reasons: (i) for a given , more lending means more leverage; and, ii) lending itself is likely to exhibit diminishing marginal returns. Hence, average asset quality goes down too. Second, : At a given , more bank equity capital decreases deadweight losses. So, unless banks are completely safe, bank equity capital is valuable because it decreases the costs of insolvency.Expressing the cost of financial instability in such a fashion has become popular since the global financial crisis. As is discussed in Malherbe (2020), such costs can be seen as stemming from the resolution process, whose complexity is likely to increase in the shortfall (as is for instance the case in the costly verification set up of Townsend (1979)). Other possible micro-foundations include the tax distortions of associated bailouts (see, e.g., Acharya et al. (2010)): given our tie breaking rule for incorporation, the shortfall is the deposit insurance bill at Home and so corresponds to the direct fiscal cost of bank failures. More generally, the specification could capture any other channel by which bank financial distress affects the real economy (Hoggarth et al., 2002). From this point of view, our approach to the cost of bank financial distress is also similar to many papers in the systemic risk literature.12The partial derivative, with respect to , of the right side of (11), defines the social net present value of the marginal loan: The intrinsic net present value from lending is : the marginal product of capital net of an opportunity cost of funds of 1. The social NPV also accounts for the deadweight losses generated by the marginal loan, . Banks do not internalise deadweight losses, and they benefit from the implicit subsidy from deposit insurance. This is why the market equilibrium generally does not implement , and constitutes a rationale for capital regulation.Solution concepts.We consider both competition and collaboration between regulators. When they collaborate, the regulators jointly set  to maximise their combined objective such that (12)When two regulators compete, we consider a symmetric Nash equilibrium of the game in which they separately maximise  and , setting their capital requirement taking the other’s as given. Formally, a Nash equilibrium is defined as a fixed point Henceforth, functions with superscripts  are evaluated at the symmetric Nash equilibrium of the competitive regulator game. For example, . Likewise, functions with superscripts  are evaluated at the market equilibrium where both capital requirements are set at .Condition 2 Regularity - Single CrossingFor all : , , , , and the same holds for .Condition 2 ensures that the first order condition to problem (12) uniquely pins down . It also ensures the individual competitive regulators’ first order conditions define well behaved best response function leading to a unique .134.2. Race to the top or the bottom?The main question we are now interested in is whether Nash competition leads to tighter or looser requirements than collaboration. As we will see, the capital flow described in Section 3 has a key role to play in determining how competition plays out versus collaboration.Nash inefficiency: the role of the capital flow externality.The first order condition for  reads (see the proof of Proposition 3) (13)Let us focus on the second term. It captures the effect on Foreign of a marginal increase in . First, consider the case in which a change in  attracts equity capital to Home. If , it must be that some of this capital has been siphoned off from Foreign:  (Proposition 2).14 Equity capital is valuable to Foreign. It can be levered up at rate  to fund lending with a marginal social value .15 Equity capital also decreases the expected shortfall on Foreign bank assets, that is . So the term in parenthesis is positive and the loss of capital hurts Foreign.Hence, changes in  generate an externality that operates through capital flows. And, in the case where , this externality is negative: Foreign welfare is falling in the Home requirement: . Now, if Eq. (13) holds and , then it must be that . But this precisely means that the competitive Home regulator has an incentive to deviate upwards at the collaborative optimum. Part of the cost of tighter Home requirements is borne abroad and so regulatory competition leads to overly tight regulation: as we will see, when , we get , which we interpret as a race to the top.However, when  the sign of the capital flow externality flips. We have  because a higher home requirement then leads to an inflow of equity capital to Foreign. More capital boosts lending and reduces the expected shortfall, so tighter Home regulation acts as a positive externality. In this case, at the collaborative optimum, the positive externality, , implies . The competitive Home regulator has an incentive to deviate towards looser regulation: when , we get , which we interpret as a race to the bottom.Whether competition leads to a race to the top or the bottom depends on the sign of : the direction of the capital flow following a unilateral deviation towards tighter regulation (starting from collaboration). From Theorem 1, we know that the sign of  depends on whether  is greater than or less than the relevant return maximising requirement.We now state this result formally:Proposition 3Assume Conditions 1 and 2 hold. Then, there are three cases where  is the fixed point , with  defined in Theorem 1.The broad intuition for the proposition is straightforward. The relation between  and  in the three cases follows the reasoning above: the incentive to deviate from collaboration depends on the sign of the capital flow. If the collaborative requirement is less than the return maximising one () a unilateral deviation from collaboration towards tighter regulation is optimal as a tighter regulation generates capital inflows (). So  and vice versa.That  places a bound on how far  can deviate from  is perhaps less important but it is also an intuitive result. Take the case  and imagine that (out of equilibrium) both regulators picked . By definition of , there is no capital flow externality from a marginal deviation. So, individual and collective incentives are the same. But we know that , so the beneficial deviation should be towards a  strictly lower than . So we have . Again, the symmetric reasoning applies to the other case (). Finally, that  when  is clear as the externality is nil at the collaborative optimum so there is no incentive to deviate.Now, whether  is greater or smaller than  depends on the primitives of the model: parameters  and the distribution . Making specific assumptions on some of them allows to derive intuition on the role of the others. This will be our strategy for characterising the Nash equilibrium further.RemarkWith our specified objective function (11), the capital flow externality emerges, in part, because a reallocation of equity capital between countries affects the expected shortfall from bank defaults. However, regulatory objectives could be modelled in a variety of ways. We show in Appendix B.2 that objective (11) is a specific case of a more general class of objective function that still results in Proposition 3. In particular, for the Proposition to hold, it is sufficient to have an objective where (i) Foreign capital requirement only matters for Home welfare through the equity capital of Home banks, , and (ii) at the collaborative optimum, given , an additional unit of equity financing is welfare enhancing.16The second condition in the remark can be interpreted as the regulators finding bank equity capital socially valuable. Meaning that an inflow of equity capital from abroad would lead to an improvement in welfare at home, whereas siphoning capital off one’s neighbour imposes a negative externality. In the case of our specific objective, more equity capital would enable banks to lend the same amount but reduce the extent of defaults, which is welfare enhancing. The policy debate is often pitched in these terms so this condition seems to correspond to the empirically relevant case. However, we discuss a case where the social value of capital can flip sign in Section 5.3.The notion that Foreign capital regulation only matters for Home welfare through the allocation of equity capital abstracts from other potential sources of spillovers between countries from regulation. We show in Appendix B.2, that the capital flow externality identified in this section still exists and has a similar interpretation even if there are other sources of spillovers (e.g., if  is perceived as socially costly).4.3. The case with more than two countriesOur policy game can be extended to more than two countries. Take the case  and consider a number of ex-ante identical countries, each with an initial investor endowment of . The collaborative optimum is then the same as in the corresponding two-country case (i.e.,  and  would be unchanged). In addition, the direction in which regulators have an incentive to deviate would still depend on whether increases in capital requirement raise the return on equity at the collaborative optimum. However, the number of countries affects  and, in particular, its distance from .To save on notation we proceed numerically: the left panel in Fig. 3 presents an illustration. For the two-country case, it displays ,  and , which is at the intersection of the best response curves. In addition, it presents the solution for  when, rather than two countries, there is a mass of atomistic countries. The example we provide is one where  .The more countries the greater the incentive to deviate and the further the Nash equilibrium from the collaborative optimum (as indicated by the atomistic case in Panel A of Fig. 3). The reason is that countries become effectively “smaller” on a global scale and, therefore, face a more elastic inverse supply curve of bank capital. Take the case where an increase in  in a country siphons off capital from abroad. Capital flows will ensue so as to restore the no-arbitrage condition in international capital markets. As the capital outflow is spread across more and more countries, the total inflow into the deviating country (for an identical deviation) becomes greater. As a result, the deviating country can achieve an identical financial stability benefit at the cost of a lower and lower decrease in economic activity.From a collaborative point of view, each country attempts to overuse equity capital in the Nash equilibrium. Not unlike in the tragedy of the commons, this gets worse as the number of users becomes large. Such an interpretation applies to the case where , which we just discussed and can be interpreted as a race to the top. But it also applies to . In this case, competition among regulators leads to a race to the bottom instead. However, this occurs precisely because cutting capital requirements attracts capital from abroad.
article.5. Discussion and extensions: Here we discuss how our host-country rule relates to real world capital regulation under the Basel III Accords. We then turn to policy implications and empirical predictions, and draw links with the stylised facts and existing empirical literature. Last, we consider an extension of the model in which deposits offer households a liquidity service, which allows us to discuss further the robustness of our analysis.5.1. Real world capital requirementsThe current Basel III regime that coordinates real world capital regulation across countries can be approximated as a static common minimum requirement (made up of a variety of different “buffers”) plus a time varying macroprudential requirement called the countercyclical capital buffer (CCyB). Hence, we can model a Basel III capital requirements for a bank  (at time ) as follows: (18)where  is the fixed common minimum, and  are the time-varying countercyclical buffers. Under Basel III rules, the  is, in effect, set by the regulator of the borrowers’ country. This is known as the principle of reciprocity in the Basel Accords. Regardless of where bank  is located it faces the  on Home loans (and  on Foreign loans).In practice, banks have three ways to lend across borders: directly, through branches, or through subsidiaries incorporated in the country of the borrower. Direct lending and lending made through branches fall under the jurisdiction of the country where the bank is incorporated and so, de jure, the capital requirement on those loans is imposed by the bank’s local regulator. However, the Accords mandate that countries impose, on their banks, for their foreign lending, the CCyB chosen by the corresponding host country (and the host country does the same; hence reciprocity). So, de facto, branches, subsidiaries, and direct cross-border loans all face the same capital requirement set by the country where the lending takes place. This is what motivates the host-country rule of our model.Three remarks are in order. First, the CCyB cannot be set at a negative level. This is something we abstract from in our model but would be straightforward to incorporate with a constraint . Second, the Accords also stipulate a limit of 2.5% for mandatory reciprocation of the CCyB (i.e. the legal obligation to reciprocate stops when the buffer reaches 2.5%). Voluntary reciprocation is still possible and, in reality, is seen for other relevant macroprudential tools. However, no country has set the CCyB past 2.5% so far. So, the willingness to voluntarily reciprocate larger buffers cannot be judged. Third, reciprocity requires that the local regulator actually adheres to the principle. Publicly refusing to implement a policy legally mandated under the Basel accords is unlikely. A potential concern is that supervisors could be secretly lax on imposing the additional reciprocated capital requirement. But this would come with reputational and legal risks and may lead to the jurisdiction being deemed Basel non-compliant, leading to barriers for its banks international operations.20 Such a strategy would also require collusion with the bank itself. An observed lack of integrity by supervisors may hinder other interactions with the banking system with consequences for wider financial stability. (For more detail on the institutional background, see Appendix A.)5.2. Empirical predictions and policy implicationsA formal empirical analysis of the CCyB regime is beyond the scope of this paper. The short time frame since the CCyB were introduced and the relatively infrequent changes in the CCyB are significant hurdles. Still, we highlight how our model may shed light on the use of such a policy tool, and we then argue that one can also draw some inference on the sign of the capital flows based on existing literature.To set the stage, consider the following thought experiment. Imagine a world in which a race-to-the-bottom is paramount but there is a common minimum to mitigate it (in our framework, ). If countries are symmetric, CCyBs would always be nil. But CCyBs are used in practice (see Fig. 3, panel B). That a race to the top can arise in our model provides a potential explanationOther mechanisms can lead to a race to the top.21 And other reasons why CCyBs are used may of course exist, especially if countries are heterogeneous. However, our analysis leads to novel predictions. We discuss them now.5.2.1. Implication 1: CCyBs are likely to overshootIn normal times, when the banking sector is healthy and bank capital is relatively abundant ( is relatively high), we are more likely to be in a situation where national regulators have an incentive to deviate upward (). Doing so would attract capital from abroad and impose a negative externality onto other countries; this follows from Proposition 4. So national regulators have an incentive to tighten the CCyB too much in good times.22 Hence, there are gains from coordinating on smaller raises. Conversely, after a negative shock, when  is low (and  could be higher too, for instance if deadweight losses emanate from tax distortions and fiscal capacity has been impaired by the shock), regulators have incentives to cut the requirement too aggressively. Hence, there are gains from coordinating on smaller cuts.Consider a version of our model where the single period would be repeated over time, with exogenous changes in . Our model predicts that  would rise with  (in line with conventional wisdom). However, the lower  the more likely . So, the prediction is that changes in  will follow changes in  but with a greater amplitude. We illustrate this in Fig. 2.As described, the Basel accords also specify limits to the CCyB. Interestingly, through the lens of our model, these limits could mitigate strategic behaviour. In bad times, if we are in the case where , we would have bunching at  as the positivity constraint on the CCyB limits the scope for regulators to undercut one another. Whereas, in good times, if a national regulator sets the buffer above 2.5%, other regulators will not be obliged to reciprocate.Download: Download high-res image (192KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Illustration of  over the cycle.5.2.2. Implication 2: Small countries are likely to use CCyBs moreAn implication of our model with more than two countries is that smaller countries should be most active in using the CCyB.23 These countries have the greatest incentive to deviate upwards during good times and to cut during bad times as, from their perspective, the supply of bank equity capital is more elastic. While other forces may be at work, this is what we have seen in reality. The most active users of the CCyB have been small economies in Scandinavia, Hong Kong and the smaller members of the Eurozone. At the time of writing, the U.S., China and Japan have not used the buffer while Germany has varied the buffer by 25bp since its introduction. To illustrate this point more concretely in the data, the right panel of Fig. 3 plots the May 2023 level of the CCyB across the 28 members of the Basel Committee (and Norway) against the country’s share in the total of members GDP (log scale). 2023 represents a period where many countries are increasing buffers after having cut them during the pandemic and so we would expect country size to be negatively correlated with CCyB rates. That is what we see in the data. The evidence is purely suggestive, but it is worth flagging that country size alone accounts for 16% of the cross-sectional variation in CCyB rates.Download: Download high-res image (229KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. Panel A:  is denoted by the blue circle;  (for the two-country case) is denoted by the red square at the intersection of the best response curves (i.e. the red dashed lines);  (for the atomistic-countries case) is denoted by the green square further away from ; and , and its Foreign counterpart, are denoted by the grey diamonds. Panel B: May 2023 CCyB rates against the country’s share of PPP GDP of Basel Committee members (plus Norway) and a line of best fit.5.2.3. Is there empirical evidence for the sign of the capital flow?There is a substantial literature identifying the effect of capital requirements at the bank level by exploiting the heterogeneous impact of regulatory reforms, stress tests, or supervisory interventions (Francis and Osborne, 2012, Bahaj et al., 2016, Gropp et al., 2019, Imbierowicz et al., 2018, Juelsrud and Wold, 2020, De Jonghe et al., 2020). A message from these papers is that banks facing an increase in capital requirement increase their equity.24 In some studies, the response is not significantly different than zero, but we are not aware of evidence that banks, on average, reduce their levels of capital in response to higher requirements.In our model, this bank-level relationship corresponds to . Extrapolating at the aggregate level, this suggests  and, therefore, corresponds to the condition under which regulators have an incentive to deviate upwards.25 Now, if , unless bank equity capital is supplied perfectly elastically (which would correspond to  in our model), it must be that .Consistent with this prediction, there is also bank-level evidence that tightening capital requirements at home causes domestic banks to cut lending abroad (see Aiyar et al., 2014, Forbes et al., 2017), which would also correspond to  in our model (and, as above, with an extrapolation to  and, in turn, ). From a broader perspective, Buch and Goldberg (2017) provide a meta analysis showing that, in general, the tightening of prudential policies (including capital requirements) spill over to generate less lending abroad.More empirical research is required to determine the direction of aggregate equity capital flows following sector-widechanges in requirements. We also emphasise that our model predicts that the direction and intensity of the flow will vary with economic conditions. The existing empirical evidence does suggest that the direction of equity capital flows is such that higher capital requirements at Home do, on average, generate a negative externality on Foreign.5.3. Liquidity service on depositsIn our model, capital requirements bind because deposits are effectively cheaper than equity. And deposits are cheaper because they are implicitly subsidised by deposit insurance. In practice, another reason why deposits are cheaper than equity is because they provide liquidity services (Drechsler et al., 2021, Drechsler et al., 2023). This is a source of rents for banks, and the associated deposit franchise is an important element of bank decision making. In this subsection, we show that all the main mechanisms of the model go through if capital requirements are binding because of deposit liquidity services instead of deposit insurance.In this liquidity service model, households derive utility from the quantity of deposits held in domestic banks. This liquidity service is non-pecuniary, is marginally decreasing, and is independent of the financial return on deposits. Specifically, in a similar vein to Begenau (2020), we use the following utility function for the representative household in Home: where ,  is date-1 consumption and  is date-0 deposit holdings; and similarly for that in Foreign.These deposits are not insured and there are two segmented deposit markets: one in Home and one in Foreign. We impose that a bank raising deposits in a country lends in it. We also impose that the bank does not invest in storage (we come back to this assumption below). Hence, banks compete internationally for equity capital, and then choose in which country to operate.Household maximisation with respect to deposit holding provides a condition which defines an inverse supply curve for (Home) deposits: where  is the expected pecuniary gross return.26 Note that the functional form ensures that, in equilibrium,  and , so that deposits are indeed cheaper than equity capital and the capital requirement binds. Hence, as before, we have  and . Repeating the steps of our analysis in Section 3.1 and recalling , Home banks’ expected return on equity is now given by (19)The bank’s objective is otherwise identical to Eq. (3) in our model. The analysis of Section 3.2 is unaffected by this redefinition of the return on equity. Changes in capital requirements at Home still generate a capital flow in the form described in Lemma 2 and this generates a spillover on Foreign as in Proposition 2.27 As a result:Proposition 5In the liquidity service model: (i) there also exists a unique equilibrium as defined in Proposition 1 using the return on equity defined in Eq. (19) and (ii) Theorem 1 also holds under Condition 1.Theorem 1 also holds in this setting.28 The economic intuition is similar to that in Section 3.3, but with an added twist. As in our baseline model, the concavity of the production function means that banks face a downward sloping, convex demand curve for loans. Hence, constraining quantities by tightening the capital requirement (holding the bank’s cost of funds fixed) will raise the return on equity up to a point after which returns start to decline. This follows the intuition of the fictitious monopolist bank outlined previously. However, in the liquidity service model, the representative bank also faces an upward sloping, concave supply curve for deposits. So, we can also build intuition by thinking of a fictitious monopsonist bank. Both the monopolist and monopsonist rents are concave in quantities. Likewise, both lending and deposits are shrinking in the capital requirement. Hence, there exists a capital requirement which maximises the combined rent and hence the return on equity. As in our baseline model, an increase in  beyond this point causes a fall in the return on equity and hence a capital outflow.The policy game in the liquidity service model.We now embed the liquidity service model into the policy game. For tractability, we consider the case where  and  follows a binary distribution:  with . As before, welfare is the value added of the banking system less deadweight losses. The difference is that the banking sector surplus now includes liquidity services. We have: . Allowing banks to lever up generates an additional gain as liquidity service provision expands.The problem of the collaborative regulators can be formulated as This yields the following first order condition (20)Compared to collaborative first order condition (14) in Section 4, the right side of Eq. (20) has an additional term reflecting that balance sheet expansion generates greater liquidity service at the margin, making lending more attractive to the regulators.Now, under Nash competition, the Home regulator first order condition is  So the externality at the collaborative optimum is given by The term  is the marginal social value of bank capital at the collaborative optimum. From Eq. (20), it is equal to the net present (private) value of the marginal loan at the collaborative optimum. For the moment, we will proceed assuming it is positive and discuss its sign further below.Under that assumption, the sign of the externality is once again pinned down by  and therefore depends on whether  is greater than, or less than, , the return maximising level of lending, where  is the level of lending that sets , that is29 Since , it directly follows that  when . As in the original model, return maximisation is associated with monopolist marginalisation. Hence, the  term appears in the equation for . What changes compared to Section 4.3.1 is that the factor  (which emanated from the rents associated with deposit insurance) is replaced by an additive term associated with monopsonist marginalisation. Since both monopolistic and monopsonistic marginalisation push in the same direction (lower quantities than what maximises social welfare), we have that . Deadweight losses still generate a force that reduces  relative to . Hence, adding a deposit franchise that yields rents for the banking sector generates similar insights to having rents from a lending franchise. A reduction in the deposit supply elasticity, , increases the likelihood that , in a similar manner to a reduction in the loan demand elasticity, .Note also that  is increasing in  and that, for ,  is decreasing in . Therefore, the same intuition around Proposition 4 still holds and so higher  makes it more likely  and vice versa.30A difference between our main model and the liquidity service model is that, in the latter, whether , depends on parameters. If deposit supply is sufficiently inelastic, the sign can switch as the regulator sees a high marginal benefit of more deposits. Equity capital funding is then, at the margin, a welfare reducing source of funds. Such a situation may however, have limited empirical relevance. The post-crisis policy debate surrounding capital regulation has focused on the trade off between tighter regulation and lending, not tighter regulation and banks ability to provide deposits (see for example BCBS (2022), Chapter 8). Second, in reality, banks have access to safe assets such as reserves and government bonds that do not generate a capital charge. Here, we assume banks cannot invest in the storage technology (which carries a zero capital requirement). If we dropped this assumption, banks would raise deposits and invest in storage until any markdown on deposits had been competed away. Capital regulation would then only be relevant for lending. Reintroducing deposit insurance would take us back to our main model.The bottom line here is that our findings do not hinge on the reason why deposits have a funding advantage. However, from a modelling perspective, the benefit of considering deposit insurance, or government guarantees in general, is that they give banks an incentive to specialise, and so their portfolio choice is determined endogenously. Absent deposit insurance, we would require the explicit, somewhat arbitrary, assumption that an individual bank will always raise deposits and lend in the same country.
article.6. Conclusion: Under a host-country rule for bank capital regulation, the relevant strategic interaction becomes competition for scarce bank equity capital. Depending on economic conditions, a rise in a given country’s capital requirement can generate capital flows of either sign. Outflows from that country are associated with a positive externality on other countries, inflows with a negative one.A key policy implication of our results is that this capital flow externality is likely to make CCyBs overshoot relative to what is socially optimal. Individual regulators raise requirements too much (compared to collaboration) in normal and good times and cut them too much in bad times.An important ingredient in our analysis is that bank equity capital is supplied somewhat inelastically and equity markets are integrated between countries. This generates the inflows and outflows described above. If bank equity capital were supplied perfectly elastically, the gains from coordination would vanish. The elasticity of the global bank equity supply is an important object as it governs gains from coordination. Future research estimating this parameter could prove fruitful.Likewise, the gains from collaboration would vanish if we endow the regulators with as many instruments as target variables (two in our case,  and ); an application of the Tinbergen (1952) principle. For instance, if a regulator could subsidise (without additional distortion) the allocation of bank capital to their country, it could both reduce leverage (via the capital requirement) and avoid the associated contraction in economic activity (via the subsidy). The assumption that such subsidy is, otherwise, not distortive is however rather extreme, and unlikely to be satisfied in practice. More generally, this feature of our model relates to Korinek (2016), which shows that violations of the Tinbergen principle can create scope for collaboration.31
